title: Multi-Agent Systems Fail 87% of the Time. We Ran 400 Real Handoffs Through a Coordination Layer. Here's What We Found.
brief: Multi-agent AI fails at 80-87% in every recent academic study, and the failures aren't about model quality — they're about coordination. I built Lattice, an open-source coordination layer (state contracts + tiered circuit breakers + pipeline orchestration), and ran 400 real handoffs through it in shadow mode in my content pipeline. The 93% pass rate is the boring number. The interesting number is that 91 of 100 L2 validations escalated to L3 — meaning my L2 layer was almost useless for creative steps. The post is about what we learned by publishing the honest data instead of the flattering version, and why the threading-libraries analogy turns out to be the right framing for multi-agent coordination.
thesis: Coordination failures in multi-agent AI are architectural, not model-capability problems. Tiered validation works, but you have to design each tier for what it's actually good at — not for what you wished it did.
facts: MAST paper (NeurIPS 2025) studied 1,600+ multi-agent traces; failure rate up to 87%
Runcycles March 2026 study of production deployments: up to 87% failure rate
Talyx 2026 enterprise study: 80-90% failure rate
MAST paper finding: better models improved success rates by less than 5%
Gartner prediction: 40% of agentic AI projects will be cancelled by 2027
80% of enterprise apps now embed agents, but only 31% have anything running in production
Lattice is open source (MIT license)
Lattice provides three primitives: State Contracts, Circuit Breakers (L1/L2/L3 tiered validation), and Pipeline orchestration
L1 is JSON Schema validation, sub-200ms, zero LLM calls
L2 is embedding similarity (text-embedding-3-small, threshold 0.85), ~600ms
L3 is LLM-as-judge (gpt-4o-mini, confidence threshold 0.7), ~25s
Lattice is framework-agnostic — wraps any agent function in LangGraph, CrewAI, Mastra, or raw TypeScript/Python
Benchmark setup: 50 documentation topics, 5 agent steps per topic, 400 handoff validations total
Benchmark ran in shadow mode inside our production content pipeline (Forge)
Total wall-clock benchmark time: 1 hour 43 minutes
Zero pipelines crashed
L1 pass rate: 100%
L2 pass rate: 91.0% (91/100 actual L2 runs); 150 handoffs skipped L2 by policy
L3 confidence: 99% of calls (239/241) landed in the 0.8-1.0 confidence band
Per-step pass rates: Research 78% (39/50), Outline 94% (47/50), Drafter 87% (87/100), Reviewer 99% (99/100), Formatter 100% (100/100)
L1 mean latency <200ms, P95 <200ms
L2 mean latency 627ms, P95 9.0s, max 13.8s
L3 mean latency 25.4s, P95 63.2s, max 110.2s
Only 2 of 241 L3 calls landed in the uncertain 0.6-0.8 band
The surprise finding: 91 of 100 L2 validations escalated to L3
150 handoffs were configured to skip L2 entirely (drafter, reviewer, formatter)
Per-step L2 configuration was added mid-benchmark after the L2 finding
Right configuration: Research→Outline uses L1+L2; Outline→Draft uses L1+L3; Draft→Review uses L1+L3; Review→Format uses L1 only
State Contracts carry inputs, decisions, outputs, constraints, assumptions — full lineage per action
Each of the 50 runs produced 8 handoff validations on average (including L2/L3 escalations)
All handoffs within a run share the same traceId
Lattice packages: @heybeaux/lattice-core, @heybeaux/lattice-provider-openai, @heybeaux/lattice-adapter-mastra, lattice-langgraph (Python, merged)
Full audit log (400 entries) is public at github.com/heybeaux/ops/blob/main/reports/lattice-shadow-audit-50topics-2026-05-08.jsonl
Aggregated report is public at github.com/heybeaux/ops/blob/main/reports/lattice-benchmark-50topics-2026-05-08.json
Lattice GitHub: github.com/heybeaux/lattice
I run heybeaux — a small dev shop where AI agents do real work in production
I am building an observability dashboard next — a web UI for the audit trail
anchors: the threading-libraries analogy — multi-agent coordination has the same problems concurrent programming has had for 60 years
200 OK with garbage downstream — silent failures returning success status with broken output
the moment we saw 91/100 L2 escalating to L3 in the logs and realised L2 wasn't doing the work we'd designed it to do
the difference between L2 catches drift and L3 catches quality — they aren't substitutes
honest data > flattering version — we could have shipped the 93% number alone and looked great; the L2 finding is what actually moves the field
shadow mode — every handoff produced a State Contract, validated, logged, but did not block the pipeline
the bimodal confidence distribution from L3 — when it's confident it's very confident, almost no noise in the middle
the per-step configuration realisation — L2 for structural-consistency steps, L3 for creative steps
voice: First-person singular throughout. I built, I ran, I found. heybeaux is the shop; the builder is one person plus AI agents. Don't use the editorial 'we'.
forbidden: Do not use the editorial 'we'. First-person singular only. The team is one human plus AI agents — don't fake collective voice.
Do not invent collaborators, co-authors, or team members. heybeaux is a one-builder shop with AI agents as teammates — that's the truthful framing.
Do not invent specific customer names, customer quotes, or case studies. The shadow-mode test ran inside our own Forge pipeline, not a customer's.
Do not invent benchmark numbers beyond those in facts[]. If a number isn't here, don't make one up.
Do not invent the date the dashboard ships. We are building it next — no committed date.
Do not over-deploy whilst/mate/bloody. Aussie spellings (colour, honour, flavour) are fine; lexical markers should appear at most once or twice if naturally.
Do not invent any other study, paper, or source beyond MAST, Runcycles, Talyx, and Gartner as listed in facts[].
Do not invent personal anecdotes (no I-was-debugging-at-3am stories). The lived material here is the build itself; don't reach for unrelated biography.
structural_preferences: Lead with the honest finding, not the flattering pass rate. The 91/100 L2 escalation discovery is the hook.
The thesis line — coordination failures are architectural, not model-capability problems — should land late, after the data carries it.
Include the per-step numbers and latency data as a clear comparison; readers care about the actuals.
Threading-libraries analogy is the right frame but don't over-explain it. One paragraph max.
Close with what's next (observability dashboard) and the links. No sign-off paragraph about lessons learned.

comparison_artifact.note: User's own draft of this same essay. After Phase 3 runs, blind-compare the Ginnung output to this draft.
